figures.gloss: Slavic thunder-god — his name from PIE *Perkʷūnos — eternally striking the serpent Veles from his oak.
figures.facet: Among the best-preserved Indo-European storm names; the Baltic Perkūnas is his near-twin.

edges.note: Both inherit the reconstructed PIE storm-god's serpent-slaying myth (*Perkʷūnos vs. the serpent).
Shared Indo-European thunderer pattern: hammer/axe, oak, and the serpent at the world's root.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Thunder deities who strike the earth with lightning, heavily associated with martial prowess and high mountains.
Perkūnas and Perun both reflect *Perkwunos within the Balto-Slavic node — the closest cognate pair in the whole storm-cluster, oak and thunder shared.
GOLD SEAM: Fierce, storm-associated war deities intimately linked to the bull and invoked to secure military victory.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The active, thunderous sky-deity who directly manages the rains, fertility, and the seasonal renewal of the earth.
Sky-father storm-wielders of unrelated families converging on the same joint, both defined by the thunderbolt at the head of their pantheons.

Both supreme storm-fathers of the northern forests wielding axe and thunderbolt; Slavic contact may have coloured the Finnic image, but Perun's *Perkwunos name has no Uralic descendant — a borrowed motif at most, not shared blood.
Both are dragon-slaying storm-sovereigns of the Indo-European thunder-type; the seam is structural here, the firmer cognate running through the *Perkwunos reconstruction.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are fierce, atmospheric warrior deities who commanded the absolute martial devotion of their respective tribes.
GOLD SEAM: the thunder-god as guarantor of oaths and smiter of the false — Slavic Perun and Igbo Amadioha, the storm wielded as the impartial arm of cosmic law across wholly unrelated families.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are violent, mountaintop storm-wielders whose cultural imprint was so strong they named the local word for Thursday.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A warrior deity who oversees oaths, protects treaties, and mediates between humanity and the cosmic order.

Two thunder-and-rain high gods who give the storm and the water on which life depends; both ride the cloud and answer the prayer for rain. Functional kin of the storm-wielder office across unrelated families, never descent.

Both name the storm and the thunder as a high honoured power; Pillán's facet is that the bolt and the eruption are the powerful dead themselves, not Perun's distinct sky-warrior — storm-wielding ancestor against storm-wielding god.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are dynamic, axe-wielding thunderers who serve as the beloved, active protectors of their respective peoples.

Hammer-and-bolt thunder-gods of the northern forests who smite the powers of evil; a structural rhyme with the Slavic storm-father, but Perun's *Perkwunos name leaves no Uralic descendant — the pattern shared, not the blood.
GOLD SEAM: Fierce, militant storm-wielding deities of the Balkans and Eastern Europe who engage in direct physical combat.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The tempest deity who commands lightning and serves as the active, martial force of the skies.

dossier.essence: The pre-Christian religion of the Slavic peoples of eastern and southeastern Europe, from the first millennium CE until Christianization — the baptism of Kievan Rus in 988 and the gradual conversion of the rest. No native account survives, so it must be reconstructed from Byzantine, Arab, and Latin chronicles, later East-Slavic sermons against paganism, place-names, folklore, and comparative Indo-European evidence.
dossier.cosmos: The evidence is fragmentary and no unified system is securely attested. At its head stands a thunder-god, Perun — heir to the Indo-European storm-deity *Perkʷūnos — whose reconstructed adversary is the serpentine, cattle-and-underworld god Veles; the Rus' Primary Chronicle records Prince Vladimir erecting a pantheon at Kiev in 980 (Perun, Khors, Dazhbog, Stribog, Simargl, and the goddess Mokosh). Scholars posit an opposition of sky and underworld and a strong ancestor cult, but a coherent doctrine of the afterlife is not reliably preserved.
dossier.practice: Worship centered on open-air sanctuaries, carved idols, and sacred groves, springs, and hilltops, with sacrifice and seasonal feasts tied to the farming year. The best-described institution is the western, Baltic-Slavic temple cult — the sanctuary of Sventovit at Arkona on Rügen, with its great idol and oracle, destroyed by the Danes in 1168. Ancestor veneration and household spirits persisted for centuries beneath official Christianity as folk custom.
dossier.canon: None native; the sources are external and later — the Rus' Primary Chronicle, Procopius, Ibn Fadlan, Thietmar of Merseburg, and Saxo Grammaticus and Helmold on the Baltic Slavs — supplemented by anti-pagan sermons and by ethnographic folklore.
dossier.reflects: A faith known almost only from the outside — the storm-god at the summit and the long afterlife of ancestral and seasonal custom beneath an imposed Christianity.
sources: Judith Kalik & Alexander Uchitel, 'Slavic Gods and Heroes' (2019)
